==========================================================================================
LINE 61 TITLE: South Africa Crush India in T20 World Cup Clash
SOURCE: www.pratidintime.com DATE: 2026-02-22
South Africa handed India a crushing 76-run defeat in their ICC T20 World Cup Super Eight clash at the Narendra Modi Stadium in Ahmedabad, snapping the Men in Blue’s unbeaten run and severely denting their net run rate. Marco Jansen and Keshav Maharaj starred with the ball after David Miller’s explosive half-century powered the Proteas to 187/7, a total India never came close to chasing. Chasing 188, India were bowled out for 111 in 18.5 overs as Jansen returned figures of 4/22 and Maharaj claimed 3/24 in a decisive display. South Africa Recovers From Early Collapse Put in to bat, South Africa stumbled early and were reduced to 20/3 inside four overs. Jasprit Bumrah struck twice, removing Quinton de Kock and Ryan Rickelton, while Arshdeep Singh dismissed skipper Aiden Markram. At 13/2 and later 20/3, the Proteas were under pressure. However, David Miller and Dewald Brevis rebuilt the innings with a commanding 97-run stand for the fourth wicket. Miller launched a calculated counterattack, bringing up his half-century in just 26 deliveries. Brevis contributed 45 off 29 balls, striking three fours and three sixes before Shivam Dube broke the partnership. Miller continued the charge, scoring 63 off 35 balls with seven boundaries and three sixes before Varun Chakaravarthy ended his stay. A late unbeaten 44 from Tristan Stubbs ensured South Africa finished strongly. Hardik Pandya conceded 20 runs in the final over as the Proteas closed at 187/7. For India, Bumrah stood out with 3/15, surpassing Ravichandran Ashwin’s 32-wicket tally to become India’s highest wicket-taker in T20 World Cup history. Arshdeep returned 2/28, while Varun and Dube picked up a wicket each. India’s Top-Order Collapse India’s pursuit unravelled almost immediately. Aiden Markram removed Ishan Kishan for a four-ball duck in the opening over. In the next over, Marco Jansen dismissed Tilak Varma for one, reducing India to 5/2 within seven deliveries. Abhishek Sharma briefly countered with a 12-ball 15, including a six off Kagiso Rabada, but fell attempting another aerial stroke. India were 26/3 in 4.3 overs and ended the powerplay at a modest 31/3. The decision to promote Washington Sundar failed to deliver as he edged behind for 11. Corbin Bosch then removed Suryakumar Yadav for 18, leaving India struggling at 51/5 before the halfway mark. Shivam Dube and Hardik Pandya attempted to stabilise the innings, taking the score to 86/5 in 14 overs. But Maharaj turned the match decisively in a single over. He dismissed Pandya for 18, removed Rinku Singh for a duck, and then sent back Arshdeep Singh, reducing India to 88/8. Dube fought a lone battle, scoring 42 off 37 balls with three sixes, but lacked support. India were eventually bowled out for 111, suffering a heavy defeat that pushed their net run rate into negative territory. Semifinal Equation Tightens The loss not only ended India’s unbeaten run in the tournament but also complicated their semifinal pathway. With net run rate taking a significant hit, India now requires convincing victories in their remaining Super Eight fixtures against Zimbabwe and West Indies to stay firmly in contention. South Africa, on the other hand, strengthened their semifinal prospects with a comprehensive performance built on middle-order resilience and disciplined bowling under pressure.

Hezbollah warned Saturday that it would have no choice but to fight on after an Israeli strike on targets in Lebanon killed eight of its operatives. Lebanon's government has vowed to disarm Hezbollah, but Israel insists it retains the right to defend itself by striking the Iran-backed militant group. On Friday, the Israeli military said it had hit Hezbollah command centres in eastern Lebanon and targets linked to the Palestinian group Hamas in the south. Hezbollah said Saturday that eight of its fighters had been killed, after Lebanon's Health Ministry said 10 people died in the east and two in the south. "What happened yesterday in the Bekaa is a new massacre and a new aggression," Hezbollah official Mahmud Qamati said, in a speech broadcast by the Al-Manar network. "What option do we have left to defend ourselves and our country? What option do we have other than resistance? We no longer have any option." Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun also condemned the attacks, which came just days after the government said the army will start implementing the second phase of its plan to disarm Hezbollah in the south of the country. The strikes came as tensions were also building between the US and Iran, with US President Donald Trump threatening military action over the Islamic republic's nuclear programme. Iran backs several armed groups in the region, including Hezbollah and Hamas. In Lebanon's eastern city of Baalbek, a mass funeral was held for commander Hussein Mohammad Yaghi and one of the fighters, with hundreds of people gathered, waving Hezbollah flags and chanting support. A Hezbollah official, speaking on condition of anonymity, told Agence France-Presse all eight members of the group were attending a meeting in the eastern Bekaa region when a strike killed them. The Israeli military said it had targeted "several terrorists of Hezbollah's missile array in three different command centres in the Baalbek area." The raids were against targets in residential areas, according to the correspondent. They came hours after an Israeli strike on the country's largest Palestinian refugee camp in the south killed two people, according to the health ministry, with Israel's army saying it had targeted Hamas. In a statement, Hamas condemned the attack, which it said led to civilian casualties as the targeted building "belongs to the joint security force charged with maintaining security and stability in the camp". Israel has kept up regular strikes on Lebanon despite a November 2024 ceasefire that sought to halt more than a year of hostilities with Hezbollah, usually saying it is targeting the group, but occasionally also Hamas militants. Aoun called Friday's attacks "a blatant act of aggression aimed at thwarting diplomatic efforts" by the US and other nations to establish stability. Washington is one of five members of a multinational committee overseeing the ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah, with the body scheduled to meet again this week. Hezbollah lawmaker Rami Abu Hamdan said the group "will not accept the authorities acting as mere political analysts, dismissing these as Israeli strikes we have grown accustomed to before every meeting of the committee." He called on Beirut to "suspend the committee's meetings until the enemy ceases its attacks." Lebanon's government last year committed to disarming the group, with the army saying last month it had completed the first phase of the plan covering the area near the Israeli border. Israel, which accuses Hezbollah of rearming since the war, has called the Lebanese army's progress on disarming the militant group insufficient. --- 518 Oman confirms US-Iran talks will take place in Geneva on Thursday ---
keys dict_keys(['id', 'title', 'source', 'date', 'date_publish', 'date_modify', 'url', 'content', 'authors', 'description'])
source www.aljazeera.com
date_publish 2026-02-22
url https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/22/oman-confirms-us-iran-talks-will-take-place-in-geneva-on-thursday?traffic_source=rss
len content 3677
Oman’s Foreign Minister Badr Albusaidi has confirmed that further talks between the United States and Iran will take place on Thursday amid spiralling tensions between the two countries. “Pleased to confirm US-Iran negotiations are now set for Geneva this Thursday, with a positive push to go the extra mile towards finalizing the deal,” Albusaidi said in a social media post on Sunday. The announcement comes as the US continues to bolster its military assets in the region raising concern about an all-out war against Iran. Hours before Oman’s announcement, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Tehran was ready to put in place a “full monitoring mechanism” to guarantee the peaceful nature of its nuclear programme and ease tensions. Asked by Face the Nation moderator Margaret Brennan why Iran would want to pursue enrichment on its soil rather than buy enriched uranium from abroad, given the US military build-up and risk of an escalation, Araghchi said the issue was a matter of “dignity and pride” for Iranians. “We have developed this technology by ourselves, by our scientists, and it is very dear to us because we have created it – we have paid a huge expense for that,” he said. Araghchi cited among the costs two decades of US sanctions, the targeted killings of Iranian scientists and US-Israeli attacks on nuclear facilities in July. “We’re not going to give [our nuclear programme] up; there is no legal reason to do that while everything is peaceful and safeguarded” by the United Nations nuclear watchdog, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). As a “committed member” of the Non-Proliferation Treaty (NPT), which mandates nonnuclear-weapon states not to seek or acquire nuclear weapons, Iran is “ready to cooperate with the agency in full”, Araghchi added. But under the treaty, Tehran also has “every right to enjoy a peaceful nuclear energy, including enrichment.” “Enrichment is a sensitive part of our negotiations, the American team knows about our position and we know their position, we have already exchanged our concerns and I think a solution is achievable,” the minister said. Enrichment is the process of isolating and garnering a rare variant, or isotope, of uranium that can produce nuclear fission. At low levels, enriched uranium can power electric plants. If enriched to approximately 90 percent, it can be used for nuclear weapons. US officials, including President Donald Trump, have previously suggested that Washington is seeking “zero enrichment” by Tehran. Earlier this month, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said any deal with Iran would need to include agreements on ballistic missiles and support for its allies in the region. Araghchi, however, said on Sunday that Iran was “negotiating only nuclear” at the present time. “There is no other subject,” he told CBS News, adding that he was optimistic that a deal could be reached. The second round of nuclear talks concluded in Geneva on February 17. The US and Iran also held indirect talks in Oman earlier this month. The Iranian delegation is working ahead of the meeting to present a draft that included “elements which can accommodate both sides’ concerns and interests” to reach a “fast deal”, Araghchi said. The top Iranian diplomat added the agreement would likely be “better” than the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA), negotiated by former US President Barack Obama in 2015. “There are elements that could be much better than the previous deal,” he said, without eleborating. “Right now there is no need for too much detail, but we can agree on our nuclear program to remain peaceful forever and at the same time, for more sanctions [to be] lifted.”

--- 648 USS Gerald R Ford creates rare two-carrier presence in Middle East ---
keys dict_keys(['id', 'title', 'source', 'date', 'date_publish', 'date_modify', 'url', 'content', 'authors', 'description'])
source www.foxnews.com
date_publish 2026-02-20
url https://www.foxnews.com/politics/what-americas-most-powerful-warship-brings-middle-east-iran-tensions-surge
len content 5376
The Pentagon is deploying USS Gerald R. Ford to the Middle East, creating a rare two-carrier presence in the region as tensions with Iran rise and questions swirl about possible U.S. military action. The Ford will reinforce USS Abraham Lincoln already operating in the theater, significantly expanding American airpower at a moment of heightened regional uncertainty. While officials have not announced imminent action, the dual-carrier presence increases the Pentagon’s flexibility — from deterrence patrols to sustained strike operations — should diplomacy falter. The largest aircraft carrier in the world The Gerald R. Ford is the largest and most advanced aircraft carrier ever built. Commissioned in 2017, the nuclear-powered warship stretches more than 1,100 feet and displaces more than 100,000 tons of water. It serves as a floating air base that can operate in international waters without relying on host-nation approval — a key advantage in politically sensitive theaters. Powered by two nuclear reactors, the ship has virtually unlimited range and endurance and is designed to serve for decades as the backbone of U.S. naval power projection. WORLD'S LARGEST AIRCRAFT CARRIER HEADS TO MIDDLE EAST AS IRAN NUCLEAR TENSIONS SPIKE DRAMATICALLY How much airpower does it carry? A typical air wing aboard the Ford includes roughly 75 aircraft, though the exact mix depends on mission requirements. Those aircraft can include F/A-18 Super Hornets, stealth F-35C Joint Strike Fighters, EA-18G Growler electronic warfare jets, E-2D Hawkeye early warning aircraft and MH-60 helicopters. In a potential conflict with Iran, several of those platforms would be central. The F-35C is designed to penetrate contested airspace and carry out precision strikes against heavily defended targets. The Growler specializes in jamming enemy radar and communications — a critical capability against Iran’s layered air defense systems. The E-2D extends surveillance hundreds of miles, helping coordinate air and missile defense. Together, they give commanders options ranging from deterrence patrols to sustained strike operations. Built for higher combat tempo What separates the Ford from earlier carriers is its ability to generate more sorties over time. Instead of traditional steam catapults, it uses an electromagnetic aircraft launch system, or EMALS, allowing aircraft to launch more smoothly and at a faster pace. The system is designed to reduce stress on jets and increase operational tempo. The ship also features advanced arresting gear and a redesigned flight deck that allows more aircraft to be staged and cycled efficiently. In a high-intensity scenario — particularly one involving missile launches or rapid escalation — the ability to launch and recover aircraft quickly can be decisive. How it compares to the Lincoln While both the Ford and the Abraham Lincoln are 100,000-ton, nuclear-powered supercarriers capable of carrying roughly 60 aircraft to 75 aircraft, they represent different generations of naval design. The Lincoln is a Nimitz-class carrier commissioned in 1989 and part of a fleet that has supported decades of operations in the Middle East. The Ford is the Navy’s next-generation carrier and the lead ship of its class. The key differences are efficiency and output. The Ford was built to generate a higher sustained sortie rate using its electromagnetic launch system, along with a redesigned flight deck and upgraded power systems. In practical terms, both ships bring substantial strike capability — but the Ford is designed to launch and recover aircraft faster over extended operations, giving commanders greater flexibility if tensions escalate. Q 172888 2026-02-28 Who will be named the third member of Iran’s temporary three-member governing council by March 2, 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-02-22. Iran has constitutional mechanisms for temporary governance during a leadership crisis. If an interim ruling council is publicly formed, observers will watch closely to see which senior cleric is chosen for its final seat.
  • Source of Truth: Official Iranian government or state-media announcements, or consistent reporting from major international outlets such as Reuters, AP, or Al Jazeera published by March 2, 2026.
  • Resolution Date: This question resolves when the third member of Iran’s temporary three-member governing council is publicly identified, no later than March 2, 2026.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the person’s full English-transliterated name as given in the confirming report.
Q 635419 2026-02-28 Which Guardian Council member will serve on Iran’s interim leadership council in March 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-02-20. Iran’s constitution provides for a temporary leadership arrangement if the office of supreme leader becomes vacant. One seat in that arrangement can go to a member of the Guardian Council until a successor is chosen.
  • Source of Truth: Official Iranian announcements identifying the members of the interim leadership council, including releases carried by IRNA or other official state institutions, corroborated by at least one major international news outlet such as Reuters or AP.
  • Resolution Date: This question resolves on the first date in March 2026 when the membership of the interim leadership council is publicly identified. If no such official identification occurs by 31st March 2026, the question is unresolved.
  • Accepted Answer Format: The full personal name of the individual filling the Guardian Council seat on the interim leadership council, in English transliteration, without honorifics. Minor spelling variants are acceptable only if they unambiguously refer to the same person.
Q 560525 2026-03-01 By March 3, 2026, which country will be identified as having mistakenly shot down three US F-15E fighter jets during an Iranian attack? Question Start Date: 2026-02-24. US warplanes and regional air-defence systems are active around the Gulf as hostilities intensify. In fast-moving air attacks, military identification errors can lead to friendly-fire incidents involving partner countries.
  • Source of Truth: Resolve using official statements from US Central Command or the relevant national military, with Reuters or AP reporting published by March 3, 2026 used as backup confirmation if needed.
  • Resolution Date: The question resolves on March 3, 2026, when public reporting identifies the country responsible for the mistaken shootdown.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the short English name of the country only.
Q 765155 2026-03-04 Which capital city will see homes near the US Embassy ordered evacuated during the regional conflict by 6th March 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-02-25. Gulf governments are monitoring rising tensions involving Iran, Israel, and US positions in the region. If the conflict spreads, authorities may order protective evacuations near diplomatic sites.
  • Source of Truth: An official government announcement, or reporting on or before 6th March 2026 from Reuters, AP, or Al Jazeera, that states homes near a US Embassy in a capital city were ordered evacuated.
  • Resolution Date: This question resolves on the first date such reporting clearly identifies the capital city, no later than 6th March 2026.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the English name of the capital city only. The answer should be a city name, not a country name.
Q 59501 2026-02-28 Which Gulf country will be described by March 18, 2026, as receiving more than half of Iran’s missiles and drones fired at Gulf countries? Question Start Date: 2026-02-27. As the regional war expands, several Gulf countries are at risk of missile and drone spillover, and one country may emerge as the main target of those attacks.
  • Source of Truth: Conflict reporting or broadcast transcripts published by March 18, 2026, from major international outlets that summarize the distribution of Iranian missile and drone attacks across Gulf countries.
  • Resolution Date: March 18, 2026, once the relevant published report or transcript is available.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the commonly used short English country name or acronym exactly as used in the resolving source.
Q 971319 2026-03-01 What location will be reported as suffering extensive damage from Iranian missiles by March 2026 in the Gulf energy strike? Question Start Date: 2026-02-28. A missile attack on a major energy hub in the Gulf can disrupt exports and intensify regional tensions.
  • Source of Truth: Official statements from the affected government and corroborating coverage from Reuters, AP, or BBC naming the damaged site in this specific incident.
  • Resolution Date: This question resolves on the first date, no later than 31 March 2026, when the site said to have sustained extensive damage is clearly identified in public reporting.
  • Accepted Answer Format: The proper English name of the damaged site or locality, written in 1 to 3 words.
Q 209948 2026-02-27 What airspace will the airline say must safely reopen before normal flights can fully resume by March 16, 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-02-26. Because of regional fighting, some Gulf airlines tie the return of full service to safety decisions about closed airspace. Travelers are watching official advisories for the condition required before normal operations return.
  • Source of Truth: This question resolves based on the airline’s official statement describing the condition for full resumption of service, with corroboration from reputable news reporting or notices from the relevant aviation regulator if necessary.
  • Resolution Date: The question resolves when that condition is publicly stated, expected no later than March 16, 2026.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the short phrase naming the airspace exactly as stated in the airline communication.
Q 354370 2026-02-28 What missile will the United States first launch from HIMARS against Iranian targets by 31 March 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-02-01. HIMARS is a mobile US rocket and missile launcher. During active conflicts, a specific missile type can make its first combat use and later be identified in official statements or major reporting.
  • Source of Truth: Resolve using official statements from the US Department of Defense or CENTCOM, or consistent reporting from multiple major news organizations, identifying the missile type first fired from an M142 HIMARS system at Iranian targets.
  • Resolution Date: The question resolves when that first-use missile is publicly identified, no later than 31 March 2026.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the official short missile name or acronym exactly as commonly reported.
Q 232393 2026-03-01 Which country's fruit exporters will be reported as facing mounting losses from shipping disruption caused by the US-Israel war on Iran by March 17, 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-02-10. Conflict-related shipping delays can cause serious losses for exporters of perishable agricultural goods. This question asks which country will later be identified in broadcast coverage as the affected exporter.
  • Source of Truth: The question resolves using Al Jazeera’s report published on or about March 17, 2026 about fruit-export disruption linked to the war on Iran. If needed, the report’s headline, body text, video transcript, and on-screen captions may all be used.
  • Resolution Date: The resolution occurs on the calendar date when that report is first publicly available, expected by March 17, 2026.
  • Accepted Answer Format: Provide the country name exactly as stated in the report.
Q 584240 2026-03-02 Which country will Pakistan say it is ready to bring into meaningful talks with Iran to help end the war by March 30, 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-02-28. Pakistan is a regional state that could try to facilitate diplomacy if the fighting continues. A mediator may try to bring Iran together with another government for negotiations.
  • Source of Truth: Reported public remarks from Pakistan's foreign minister, confirmed by major international news coverage available by March 30, 2026.
  • Resolution Date: This question resolves on the date, no later than March 30, 2026, when Pakistan is explicitly reported as naming the country it wants in talks with Iran.
  • Accepted Answer Format: The common English short name of the country named as Iran's counterpart in the proposed talks.
Q 656436 2026-03-02 Which country will President Donald Trump threaten to cut off all trade with by 5 March 2026 for refusing US base access in the war with Iran? Question Start Date: 2026-02-19. Some allied countries host US military facilities. If one of those countries refuses to allow those bases to be used in a new war, it could trigger a serious diplomatic and economic dispute with Washington.
  • This question resolves based on public remarks from President Donald Trump, White House statements, and/or multiple major news reports by 5 March 2026 identifying the country he threatens with a total trade cutoff over denied base access.
  • 5 March 2026, or earlier once the threat is publicly reported.
  • The answer must be the country's short English name as used in the resolving source.
Q 937255 2026-03-03 Which waterway will have a cargo ship reported ablaze after being hit by an unknown projectile by March 11, 2026? Question Start Date: 2026-03-02. Maritime trade routes in the Gulf region are under close watch as the conflict expands. Commercial shipping may face security incidents at sea.
  • Statements from maritime security authorities, including UK Maritime Trade Operations or the British military, or widely cited reporting from major international news organizations available by 23:59 UTC on 11 March 2026.
  • This question resolves to the named waterway where the cargo ship is explicitly reported ablaze after being struck by an unknown projectile.
  • Provide the commonly used English name of the waterway as a short phrase of 1-3 words.
Q 833672 2026-03-14 Which country will state by March 16, 2026 that it will not participate, “not even in any effort to keep the Strait of Hormuz open by military means”? Question Start Date: 2026-02-28. The United States may ask partner countries to support naval operations aimed at protecting shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Some governments could publicly reject direct military involvement.
  • Source of Truth: an official government spokesperson statement, or matching reports from major reputable news organizations published on or before March 16, 2026.
  • Resolution Date: when that statement is first publicly reported, no later than March 16, 2026.
  • Accepted Answer Format: the common English name of the country.

import json,glob,re
terms=['Guardian Council','interim leadership council','temporary leadership council','three-member governing council','Article 111','Ahmad Jannati','Abbas Ali Kadkhodaei','Mohseni-Ejei','Ghalibaf','Larijani','Khamenei succession']
pats=[re.compile(re.escape(t),re.I) for t in terms]
for p in sorted(glob.glob('articles/2026/02/*/articles.jsonl')):
    for i,line in enumerate(open(p),1):
        a=json.loads(line); title=a.get('title') or ''; text=title+' '+(a.get('content') or '')
        hits=[terms[j] for j,patt in enumerate(pats) if patt.search(text)]
        if hits and re.search('Iran|Khamenei|Guardian|Larijani',text,re.I):
            print('\\nFILE',p,'LINE',i,'TITLE:',title,'HITS',hits[:8])
            # around first meaningful hit
            first=None
            for h in hits:
                if h not in ['Iran']:
                    first=h; break
            first=first or hits[0]
            m=re.search(re.escape(first),text,re.I)
            lo=max(0,m.start()-500); hi=min(len(text),m.end()+1000)
            print(text[lo:hi].replace('\\n',' ')[:1800])
PY"
